info.ONI:
The National Olympiad in Informatics (ONI) is Romania’s national algorithmic programming competition for pre-university students, held annually for two divisions: middle school (grades 5–8) and high school (grades 9–12). Its purpose is to evaluate algorithmic and programming skills nationwide and to identify students with potential for Romania’s international informatics teams.

Participation in ONI is based on qualification through several stages: school/local, county/regional, and finally the national stage. At the national stage, roughly around 100 contestants per grade compete, representing the top students from all across the country.

The national stage is an in-person event (except during the two COVID years when it was held online), reuniting all qualified contestants in one location. Traditionally, ONI lasted two days of competition, but since COVID it has consisted of a single day of contest, during which participants solve several challenging algorithmic problems within a few hours. Scoring is done by subtasks, following the IOI-style system where each problem is divided into multiple test groups with partial scores.

In terms of difficulty, ONI is demanding at the upper end. The hardest problems are comparable to the top tier of USACO Gold or Platinum divisions, and similar in style to Codeforces problems from Div2 C–D up to Div1 A–B, depending on the year. The middle school section is somewhat easier but still rigorous for its level.

Awards at ONI are given according to IOI-style ranking thresholds: approximately the top 1/12 of contestants receive gold medals, the top 1/4 receive silver, the top 1/2 receive bronze, and mentions are awarded by the Romanian Ministry of Education to roughly the top 15% of remaining participants. In addition to medals, ONI also traditionally designates 1st, 2nd, and 3rd prizes within each grade.

After ONI, an additional selection process is held, consisting of training camps and extra contests (called “baraje”), to form the national teams. Outstanding ONI performers qualify for the national training pool, from which four students are eventually chosen to represent Romania at the International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI), based on cumulative performance across all selection stages.

In short, ONI is the key step in Romania’s pathway to IOI, combining multi-level qualification, a high-level national contest that gathers the country’s best students in one place, IOI-style problem scoring, and a rigorous post-ONI selection system that determines Romania’s IOI representatives.

Since the pandemic, the organization and coordination of ONI have been managed by SEPI (Societatea pentru Excelență și Performanță în Informatică). More details can be found on their official website: https://sepi.ro/
info.LOT:
The LOT (national training and selection camp) is the advanced stage that follows the National Olympiad in Informatics (ONI) in Romania. It serves as the main pathway toward representing Romania at international informatics competitions such as the International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI), the Central European Olympiad in Informatics (CEOI), the Balkan Olympiad in Informatics (BOI), the Shumen Tournament, the Romanian Master of Informatics (RMI), the Junior Balkan Olympiad in Informatics (JBOI), the European Junior Olympiad in Informatics (EJOI), and the European Girls’ Olympiad in Informatics (EGOI).

The LOT is officially organized by the Romanian Ministry of Education and the national informatics committee, bringing together the top-performing students from ONI. There are two main divisions: the Senior LOT, for high school students, and the Junior LOT, for middle school students. Starting in 2025, a separate girls’ LOT has been introduced to select and prepare Romania’s representatives for EGOI.

Students are invited to the LOT based on their ONI results, typically the top 20–25 contestants per division. The camps are held in person and consist of intensive algorithmic training sessions and multiple high-level contests known as 'baraje'. These contests are scored using IOI-style subtasks, rewarding partial solutions and precision. The level of difficulty is very high — on par with top-tier international contests — and designed to test advanced problem-solving, data structures, and algorithmic optimization skills.

Performance across all LOT contests determines which students qualify for international competitions. The best four participants from the Senior LOT form Romania’s IOI team, while others may represent the country in CEOI, BOI, or other international events. Similarly, Junior LOT students are selected for contests such as JBOI or EJOI, and the new girls’ LOT serves as the gateway to EGOI.

Overall, the LOT represents the highest level of competitive programming training for Romanian students. It combines rigorous contests, national collaboration, and expert guidance to prepare the country’s top young programmers for international excellence.

Since the pandemic, the LOT and related national training activities have been coordinated by SEPI (Societatea pentru Excelență și Performanță în Informatică). More information is available on their website: https://sepi.ro/
info.IOI:
The International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI) is the most prestigious global competition in computer science and programming for high school students. It brings together contestants from over 90 countries each year to solve challenging algorithmic problems that require creativity, efficiency, and advanced reasoning. The IOI is held annually in a different host country and spans two five-hour contest days, each featuring three tasks scored using subtasks that reward partial solutions. It represents the highest level of competitive programming at the secondary education level, and medalists are recognized internationally for their excellence in computer science problem solving.

For more information, visit the official IOI statistics and archive website: https://stats.ioinformatics.org/
info.CEOI: The Central European Olympiad in Informatics (CEOI) is a regional informatics competition for high school students from Central European countries. It was created to strengthen cooperation and raise the standard of informatics education and competition in the region. The CEOI follows the same format as the IOI, with two contest days and IOI-style subtask scoring, but includes fewer participating countries. It is known for its high level of difficulty, often serving as an intermediate step between national olympiads and the IOI
info.BOI: The Balkan Olympiad in Informatics (BOI) is a regional programming contest for high school students from countries in the Balkan region and invited guests. It uses the same contest structure as the IOI, with two competitive days and subtask-based scoring. The BOI focuses on algorithmic problem solving and serves as both a preparation and qualification opportunity for IOI-level competitions. It is highly competitive and features strong participants from Southeastern Europe, with Romania being one of its founding and most successful countries.
info.EGOI: The European Girls’ Olympiad in Informatics (EGOI) is an international competition created to encourage and promote the participation of girls in competitive programming. It follows the IOI contest format, with two five-hour rounds and IOI-style subtask scoring. EGOI gathers female contestants from across Europe and invited guest countries, providing a friendly yet challenging environment that mirrors the rigor of the IOI. Romania has participated since the early editions, and starting in 2025, a dedicated girls’ LOT has been established to select and train Romania’s EGOI representatives.
info.JBOI: The Junior Balkan Olympiad in Informatics (JBOI) is a regional competition for younger students from Balkan countries and invited guests. It mirrors the structure of the senior Balkan Olympiad (BOI) but is restricted to junior contestants, usually under 15.5 years old. The contest consists of two rounds of algorithmic problems scored by subtasks, following the IOI model. JBOI provides an opportunity for junior programmers to gain early international experience and serves as a key training and motivational step toward later participation in EJOI, BOI, or IOI.
info.EJOI: The European Junior Olympiad in Informatics (EJOI) is a continental competition designed for younger students, typically under 15.5 years old, who show strong potential in algorithmic programming. It follows the IOI-style two-day format with subtask scoring and aims to give talented junior contestants early experience in international-level contests. EJOI helps bridge the gap between national junior olympiads and senior competitions such as BOI or IOI. Romania has been one of the leading participants since the contest’s inception, frequently earning top results.
